request.question: What artist created a traditional folk song among prisoners in the American South as well as Maybellene?

title: Sarie Marais
Here We à Go Go Again!
Contemporary folk music
On Top of Old Smoky
The House of the Rising Sun
Song So-hee
Folk Den
Billy Boy
Midnight Special (song)
Min'yō
sentences: "Sarie Marais" (also known as "My Sarie Marais" and pronounced "May SAH-ree mah-REH") is a traditional South African folk song, created during either the First Anglo-Boer War (c. 1880) (less likely) or the Second Anglo-Boer War (ca. 1900).

The tune was possibly taken from a song dating from the American Civil War called "Ellie Rhee" (itself perhaps a version of the traditional folk song "Foggy Dew"), with the words translated into Afrikaans.
Here We à Go Go Again!
is Johnny Rivers's second official album, and like his first album, "At the Whisky à Go Go", it was recorded live at the Whisky a Go Go in Los Angeles, California.
The album reached #38 on the Billboard Charts and spawned two hit singles: "Maybellene" and "Midnight Special".
Contemporary folk music refers to a wide variety of genres that emerged in the mid 20th century and afterwards which were associated with traditional folk music.
Starting in the mid-20th century a new form of popular folk music evolved from traditional folk music.
This process and period is called the (second) folk revival and reached a zenith in the 1960s.
The most common name for this new form of music is also "folk music", but is often called "contemporary folk music" or "folk revival music" to make the distinction.
The transition was somewhat centered in the US and is also called the American folk music revival.
Fusion genres such as folk rock, folktronica, and others also evolved within this phenomenon.

While contemporary folk music is a genre generally distinct from traditional folk music, it often shares the same English name, performers and venues as traditional folk music; even individual songs may be a blend of the two.
"On Top of Old Smokey" is a traditional folk song of the United States.
As recorded by The Weavers, the song reached the pop music charts in 1951.
It is catalogued as Roud Folk Song Index No. 414.
In one version the first verse is the following; for more on the words see below.
"The House of the Rising Sun" is a traditional folk song, sometimes called "Rising Sun Blues".
It tells of a life gone wrong in New Orleans; many versions also urge a sibling to avoid the same fate.
The most successful commercial version, recorded in 1964 by British rock group the Animals, was a number one hit on the UK Singles Chart and also in the United States and France.
As a traditional folk song recorded by an electric rock band, it has been described as the "first folk-rock hit".
Song So-hee (born October 20, 1997), is a South Korean singer who specializes in minyo, traditional Korean folk songs.
She has been active since childhood and has often been called a "young female master singer".
She became famous nationwide after she performed "Changbu Taryeong", a well-known Korean traditional folk song, at the KBS National Singing Contest.
She left a deep impression upon the viewers with her performance.
Since then she has been invited to perform at various events, both at home and abroad.
Currently she is a first-year college student at Dankook University (as a traditional music major) and successfully manages her life as a student and a performer.
Folk Den is a folk music website founded in 1995 by Roger McGuinn, former front man of The Byrds.
Hosted at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill's ibiblio, the site is intended to preserve and promote folk music and offers a new folk song on a monthly basis.
Each posting provides an MP3 of a traditional folk song along with a descriptive paragraph, lyrics, guitar chords and related images.
The site has received positive reviews from "The New York Times", the Discovery Channel, and CNET.
"Billy Boy" is a traditional folk song and nursery rhyme found in the United States.
It has a Roud Folk Song Index number of 326.
It is a variant of the traditional English folk song "My Boy Billy", collected by Ralph Vaughan Williams and published by him in 1912 as number 232 in "Novello's School Songs".
The song is very popular with the Orange Order.
"Midnight Special" is a traditional folk song thought to have originated among prisoners in the American South.
The title comes from the refrain, which refers to the passenger train "Midnight Special" and its "ever-loving light" (sometimes "ever-living light"):
Min'yō (民謡 ) is a genre of traditional Japanese music.
The term is a translation of the German word "Volkslied" (folk song) and has only been in use since the twentieth century.

Japanese traditional designations referring to more or less the same genre include "inaka bushi" ("country song") "inaka buri" ("country tune"), "hina uta" ("rural song") and the like, but for most of the people who sang such songs they were simply "uta" (song).

The term min'yō is now sometimes also used to refer to traditional songs of other countries, though a preceding adjective is needed: Furansu min'yō = French folk song; for this reason, many sources in Japanese also feel the need to preface the term with "Nihon": Nihon min'yō = Japanese [traditional] folk song.

title: Here We à Go Go Again!
Here We à Go Go Again!
Midnight Special (song)

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

gold_support_titles: Here We à Go Go Again!
response.hop1_query: What artist created a traditional folk song among prisoners in the American South as well as Maybellene?
hop1_docs: Midnight Special (song) | "Midnight Special" is a traditional folk song thought to have originated among prisoners in the American South.  The title comes from the refrain, which refers to the passenger train "Midnight Special" and its "ever-loving light" (sometimes "ever-living light"):

Song Chang-sik | Song Chang-Sik (born 2 February, 1947) is a South Korean folk singer-songwriter.  He is well known for denying the traditional Solfège (both Korean and Western), and re-interpreting the melody on his own.  These melodies can be hardly written with traditional note system, as he "creates" notes that never existed.b

Sarie Marais | "Sarie Marais" (also known as "My Sarie Marais" and pronounced "May SAH-ree mah-REH") is a traditional South African folk song, created during either the First Anglo-Boer War (c. 1880) (less likely) or the Second Anglo-Boer War (ca. 1900).  The tune was possibly taken from a song dating from the American Civil War called "Ellie Rhee" (itself perhaps a version of the traditional folk song "Foggy Dew"), with the words translated into Afrikaans.

The Hollow Trees | The Hollow Trees play what they call “folk music for Families.”  It’s high energy acoustic Americana music played with passion, skill, and humor.  They combine the musical styles of bluegrass music, country music, folk music, blues and jazz and put their stamp on many traditional folk songs as well as original compositions.

Folsom Prison Blues | "Folsom Prison Blues" is a song written in 1953 and first recorded in 1955 by American singer-songwriter Johnny Cash.  The song combines elements from two popular folk styles, the train song and the prison song, both of which Cash continued to use for the rest of his career.  It was one of Cash's signature songs.  It was the eleventh track on his debut album "With His Hot and Blue Guitar" and it was also included (same version) on "All Aboard the Blue Train".  A live version, recorded among inmates at Folsom State Prison itself, became a #1 hit on the country music charts in 1968.

Down in the Valley (folk song) | "Down in the Valley", also known as "Birmingham Jail", is a traditional country-blues American folk song.  It has been recorded by many artists, and is included in the "Songs of Expanding America" recordings in the Burl Ives six-album set "Historical America in Song".

You Are My Little Bird | You Are My Little Bird is an album by Elizabeth Mitchell, released in 2006 by Smithsonian Folkways Recordings.  The album is a collection of children's music played in a folk music style.  In addition to versions of folk tunes, it features covers of a variety of songs by other artists, among them "What Goes On" by The Velvet Underground, Neil Young's "Little Wing", and "Three Little Birds" by Bob Marley.  On most of the songs, Mitchell is accompanied by family members.  The liner notes include personal stories from the artist, but no lyrics.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- "Midnight Special (song)" — [1]: "\"Midnight Special\" is a traditional folk song"
- "Folsom Prison Blues" — [5]: "\"Folsom Prison Blues\" is a song written in 1953"
- "Johnny Cash" — [5]: "American singer-songwriter Johnny Cash"
- "Song Chang-sik" — [2]: "Song Chang-Sik (born 2 February, 1947)"
- "Sarie Marais" — [3]: "\"Sarie Marais\" (also known as \"My Sarie Marais\""
- "The Hollow Trees" — [4]: "play what they call “folk music for Families.”"
- "Down in the Valley (folk song)" — [6]: "\"Down in the Valley\", also known as \"Birmingham Jail\""
- "You Are My Little Bird" — [7]: "You Are My Little Bird is an album by Elizabeth Mitchell"

Ambiguities:
- "Midnight Special" — passage [1] labels it "a traditional folk song" (no single creator attributed).
- "Maybellene" — not present in the provided passages (no artist or page cited).
- Prison-song attribution confusion — passages include both a traditional prison-origin song ("Midnight Special") and a prison-recording hit ("Folsom Prison Blues" / Johnny Cash), which are distinct.

Bridge / missing fact:
- The passages do NOT state who created or is credited with "Maybellene".
- The passages do NOT attribute "Midnight Special" to any single creator/artist (only call it "traditional").
- Therefore it is unresolved whether any single artist "created" both "Midnight Special" and "Maybellene".

Retrieval direction:
- Open page: "Maybellene" (song) — or run query: "Maybellene song Chuck Berry 1955"
- Open page: "Midnight Special (song)" (for versions, credited arrangers, notable recordings)
- Open page: "Chuck Berry" (to confirm Maybellene authorship and any recordings of Midnight Special)
- Optional: run query: "Midnight Special Lead Belly" (to check common performer attribution)
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: Here We à Go Go Again! | Here We à Go Go Again!  is Johnny Rivers's second official album, and like his first album, "At the Whisky à Go Go", it was recorded live at the Whisky a Go Go in Los Angeles, California.  The album reached #38 on the Billboard Charts and spawned two hit singles: "Maybellene" and "Midnight Special".

The Midnight Special (radio) | The Midnight Special is a syndicated radio show broadcast on Chicago, Illinois radio station, WFMT-FM (98.7 MHz) since 1953.  It is a showcase for folk and roots music from historical and contemporary artists.  The show also features comedy sketches and show tunes.  The official description is: "folk music & farce, show tunes & satire, madness & escape."  The show is named after the well known folk song of the same name, which is often associated with Lead Belly, whose version of the song is used as the show's opening theme.  The show title also tied into its time slot, as it was broadcast on WFMT near midnight every Saturday.  It now begins at 9 p.m. and ends at midnight.

Midnight Special (song) | "Midnight Special" is a traditional folk song thought to have originated among prisoners in the American South.  The title comes from the refrain, which refers to the passenger train "Midnight Special" and its "ever-loving light" (sometimes "ever-living light"):
The Midnight Special and Other Southern Prison Songs | The Midnight Special and Other Southern Prison Songs
List of guests appearing on The Midnight Special | This is a partial list of guests who appeared on "The Midnight Special".

The Midnight Special (train) | The Midnight Special was the name of a passenger train formerly operated by the Chicago and Alton Railroad and its successor, the Gulf, Mobile and Ohio Railroad.  The train ran on an overnight schedule, and in later years carried the last regularly scheduled Pullman sleeping car between Chicago, Illinois and St. Louis, Missouri.  The train made its final run on April 30, 1971, although Amtrak continued several other passenger trains over the same route traversed by the "Midnight Special".

Maybellene | "Maybellene" is one of the first rock and roll songs.  It was written and recorded in 1955 by Chuck Berry, and inspired/adapted from the Western Swing fiddle tune "Ida Red," which was recorded in 1938 by Bob Wills and his Texas Playboys.  Berry's song tells the story of a hot rod race and a broken romance.  It was released in July 1955 as a single by Chess Records, of Chicago, Illinois.  It was Berry's first single and his first hit.  "Maybellene" is considered one of the pioneering rock songs: "Rolling Stone" magazine wrote, "Rock & roll guitar starts here."  The record is an early instance of the complete rock-and-roll package: youthful subject matter; a small, guitar-driven combo; clear diction; and an atmosphere of unrelenting excitement.  The lyrics describe a man driving a V8 Ford chasing his unfaithful girlfriend in her Cadillac Coupe DeVille.
hop2_titles: Here We à Go Go Again!
The Midnight Special and Other Southern Prison Songs
List of guests appearing on The Midnight Special
response.summary_2: "Midnight Special" is a traditional folk song thought to have originated among prisoners in the American South, while "Maybellene" was written and recorded by Chuck Berry; the provided passages give no evidence that the same artist created both songs.
